coredata.dc:title: Rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots flourescent sensor system for sensitive recognition of mercury ions
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yue
Xiao, Xuechun
Xing, Xinxin
Wang, Zhezhe
Zou, Tong
Wang, Zidong
Zhao, Rongjun
Wang, Yude
coredata.dc:description: Water-soluble rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots (RhB-GQDs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, using rhodamine B and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. The as-synthesized RhB-GQDs have few layers configuration with an average size of 4.5 nm and good distribution. The fluorescence quenching approach was applied to the sensing of mercury ions in water. Rodamine B absorb on the surface of RhB-GQDs, and it can prevent RhB-GQDs from contacting with interfering cations. The presence of N atoms reduces the ability of interfering cations to form stable complex with carboxyl groups, and gives the RhB-GQDs good selectivity to mercury ions. In the case of low concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs still has a good liner relationship with concentration. Due to the strong affinity of Hg2+ to amino and carboxyl on the surface of RhB-GQDs, the fluorescence of RhB-GQDs is quenched by nonradiative electron transfer between Hg2+ and the excited state of RhB-GQDs. Based on the fluorescence turn off process, the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs can be served as fluorescent platform for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.16 nM.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots
Fluorescence sensor
High sensitivity
Fluorescence quenching
Mercury ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by the RhB-GQDs
Detection of Hg2+ ions in real drinking water samples
Morphological and structural characterization of RhB-GQDS
Influence of pH towards the fluorescence behavior of RhB-GQDs
Detection of Hg2+ using RhB-GQDs as fluorescence probe
Mechanisms of the Hg2+ sensing properties of RhB-GQDs
Selective fluorescence response of RhB-GQDs for Hg2+ detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province via the Key Project for the Science and Technology (Grant No. 2017FA025 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61761047 and 41876055 ) and Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Yunnan Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM, (b) HRTEM, (c) particle size distribution (n = 40), and (d) AFM images of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs.
The XPS (a) survey scan and deconvoluted (b) C 1s, (c) N 1s and (d) O 1s peaks of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs.
The optical properties of the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs. (a) UV–vis and fluorescence spectra, (b) the fluorescence spectra of RhB-GQDs excited with various wavelengths ranging from 250 to 400 nm.
The pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs before and after the addition of 1 μM Hg2+ at various pH values of 2–10.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of RhB-GQDs upon addition of various concentration of Hg2+ ranging from 0 to 50 μM, (b) the change in fluorescence intensity as a function of Hg2+ concentration, the linear calibration plot of Hg2+ detection in the range of 1–10 nM (c) and 50–1000 nM (d).

(a) Fluorescence decay curves of RhB-GQDs in the absence (black curve) and presence (red curve) of 10 μM Hg2+. (b) UV–vis absorption spectra of RhB-GQDs (black line), in the presence of 1 μM Hg2+ (red line) and 3 μM Hg2+ (blue line).
The selectivity of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs for Hg2+ over various interfering metal ions.
Schematic diagram for RhB-GQDs fluorescence quenching by Hg2+ ions.
The fluorescence intensity ratio as a function of Hg2+ concentration in real sample detection.
Comparison of different fluorescent probes for Hg2+ detection.
Recovery of Hg2+ detection in drinking water samples.

head.ce:title: Rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots flourescent sensor system for sensitive recognition of mercury ions

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People's Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People's Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab of Quantum Information of Yunnan Province, Yunnan University

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University, 650091 Kunming, People's Republic of China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering, Yunnan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-soluble rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots (RhB-GQDs) were synthesized by a facile one-pot hydrothermal method, using rhodamine B and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. The as-synthesized RhB-GQDs have few layers configuration with an average size of 4.5 nm and good distribution. The fluorescence quenching approach was applied to the sensing of mercury ions in water. Rodamine B absorb on the surface of RhB-GQDs, and it can prevent RhB-GQDs from contacting with interfering cations. The presence of N atoms reduces the ability of interfering cations to form stable complex with carboxyl groups, and gives the RhB-GQDs good selectivity to mercury ions. In the case of low concentration, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs still has a good liner relationship with concentration. Due to the strong affinity of Hg2+ to amino and carboxyl on the surface of RhB-GQDs, the fluorescence of RhB-GQDs is quenched by nonradiative electron transfer between Hg2+ and the excited state of RhB-GQDs. Based on the fluorescence turn off process, the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs can be served as fluorescent platform for the sensitive and selective detection of Hg2+ with a detection limit of 0.16 nM.

Rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots (RhB-GQDs) synthesized a facile one-pot hydrothermal method using rhodamine B and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source exhibit the excellently selective and sensitive response to Hg2+ as flourescent sensor.
fx1

ce:keyword.ce:text: Rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Mercury is widely known as quicksilver and its compounds had been used as colorant in ancient China. Mercury ions are one of the heavy metal ions, and their high toxicity to the human body and environment has greatly drawn people's attention. Although mercury is highly toxic and potentially hazardous to human and environment, but it has broad employment in many industries, such as mercury battery, mercury-vapor lamps and mercury switch. There are two main ways for mercury emissions, natural and anthropogenic emissions, while the anthropogenic emissions are the main source of mercury pollution. There are three common forms of mercury poisoning, by exposure to water-soluble forms of mercury, by inhalation of mercury vapor, or by ingestion any form of mercury.

In recent years, people have a clear understanding of the toxicity of mercury ions [1,2]. Mercury can leads to serious healthy issue like headaches, renal failure, hyperspasmia and so on [3]. In order to prevent mercury poisoning, developing effective and fast analytical methods for the sensitive detection of Hg2+ is of great significance. Take into account this requirement, several analytical technologies have been developed to determine Hg2+, such as inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICPMS) [4], atomic absorption spectrometry [5] and surface enhanced Raman scattering [6]. Although those methods offer high accuracy, but the most common methods require highly expensive and complex instruments, time consuming pretreatment, and sophisticated operations, which limit the practical application of these methods for rapid on-site analysis. Recently, nanomaterials like CdTe and CdS quantum dots have been used as sensitive sensor to analyze hazardous substances [7,8]. The inorganic semiconductive quantum dots have several advantages such as low-cost, fast response, high selectivity and sensitivity [9–11]. However, the high toxicity and bioaccumulation of Cd2+ to human beings make people more cautious about the use of cadmium-containing quantum dots, and limit the application of cadmium-containing quantum dots in heavy metal ions detection. In order to prevent the risks that mentioned above, the development of non-toxicity quantum dots as fluorescence sensor to detect heavy metal ions like Hg2+ is imperative. Hence, carbonaceous nanomaterials carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have been paid immense attention due to their excellent optical properties, low toxicity, environmentally friendly, and good biocompatibility [12–15]. GQDs have been developed to detect hydrogen peroxide and glutathione [16–18]. Besides that various fluorescent sensors have been applied to determine heavy metal ions based on the outstanding fluorescence, easy synthesis and functionalization of GQDs [19–22]. Although several advantages are accounted, the sensitivity of GQDs is relying on the functional group on the surface. Therefore, surface functionalization and doping of GQDs with heteroatoms have been undertaken to improve the sensitivity to heavy metal ions and toxic object [23–26]. Rhodamine B derivative-functionalized graphene quantum dots have been applied as fluorescence turn on sensor for the Fe3+ detection [27]. By the assistance of rhodamine B derivative, the sensitivity and selectivity of graphene quantum dots for Fe3+ can be improved. Herein, we introduce rhodamine B into graphene quantum dots to improve its selectivity on Hg2+ detection.

The high toxicity and bioaccumulate of mercury can be harmful to creatures at very low concentration. Animal studies show that low concentration of mercury (0.2 μg/g) can lead to cell damage and death [28]. Therefore, low concentration detection of high toxicity mercury ions is more important, which requires good linear relations and low detection limits at low concentrations. In this paper, rhodamine B assisted graphene quantum dots (RhB-GQDs) were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method, using rhodamine B and ethylenediamine as nitrogen source. The as-synthesized RhB-GQDs have good sensitive recognition for mercury ions in the presence of high concentration of interfering metal ions. The quenching mechanism of RhB-GQDs-Hg2+ assembly is a typical electron transfer process. The sensitive recognition of RhB-GQDs to mercury ions is related to the good affinity of mercury ions on amino and carboxyl groups. Rhodamine B can not only serve as nitrogen source in the formation process of RhB-GQDs, but also can serve as protective agent. Rodamine B can absorb on the surface of the RhB-GQDs and form a protective layer to prevent RhB-GQDs from contacting with interfering cations. The presence of N atoms is conductive to reduce the interaction between interfering cations and carboxyl. Thus, only mercury ions can lead to effective fluorescence quenching. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs to mercury ions have good linear relations and highly sensitive at nanomolar level concentration detection with a low detection limit of 0.16 nM.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, Rhodamine B, sodium hydroxide, disodium hydrogen phosphate, sodium dihydrogen phosphate, phosphoric acid and metal salts were purchased from commercial sources and used without any further purification.

RhB-GQDs were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method by using critic acid as the carbon source. In brief, 0.5 g critic acid 0.0116 g rhodamine B and 400 µL ethylenediamine were dissolved into 50 mL of ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) under stirring condition to form a claret-colored solution. The mixture was subsequently transferred into 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave tube and heated for 10 h at 180 °C. The obtained red suspension was centrifuged at 8000 rpms for 20 min to remove of the large size carbon dots. After the centrifugation the obtained supernatant was collected to dialyze using a dialysis bag with a cutoff molecular weight of 3500 Da for 10 h.

The morphological and structural properties of RhB-GQDs were characterized with Uv–vis–1800 spectrophotometer (Jinghua Instrument, China), JEOL-JEM 2100 Transmission electron microscope (TEM), AVATAR360 Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) spectrometer, K-Alpha+ X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Seiko SPA-400 SPM Atomic force microscope (AFM). The photoluminescence properties were recorded by Horiba Fluorolog-3 fluorescence spectrophotometer and the emission slit width set as 3 nm. The time resolved photoluminescence lifetime decays were carried out by Hamamatsu compact fluorescence lifetime spectrometer C1167, using an LED light source with λ
ex = 365 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ by the RhB-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectra were recorded at 450 nm with an excitation wavelength of 350 nm, after incubation with PBS (phosphate buffered saline) buffer (0.1 M, pH 7) and different concentration of Hg2+ for 10 min at room temperature. Initially, 50 µL RhB-GQDs solution (0.5 mg mL−1) and 750 µL PBS (0.1 M, pH 7) were mixed, and then 200 µL various concentrations of Hg2+ were added into the mixed solution. Following that, the resulting solution was diluted with ultra-pure water to 5 mL. The final Hg2+ concentration was on the range from 1 nM to 50 µM. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was performed by recording the change in relative fluorescence intensity (I/I0) in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively. The selectivity of RhB-GQDs was recorded at the same condition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ ions in real drinking water samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the applicability of RhB-GQDs in real water samples, three drinking water samples were purchased from commercial source (Nongfu Spring, Nongfu spring co. LTD, China). 50 nM, 100 nM and 300 nM Hg2+ were added into drinking water samples followed by the addition of 0.5 mg mL−1 RhB-GQDs. The applicability of RhB-GQDs were evaluated by the recovery of Hg2+ in drinking water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of RhB-GQDS
ce:section.ce:para: TEM and AFM images of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs are shown in Fig. 1. The TEM images reveal that the size of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs is distributed in the range of 2.5–6.5 nm, with an average size of 4.5 nm. Fig. 1(b) depicts the HRTEM image of RhB-GQDs. The obvious crystal lattice can be seen and the fringes of carbon lattice spacing distance is measured as 2.16 Å, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphene [25]. The thickness of RhB-GQDs was cautiously measured by AFM (Fig. 1(d)). The maximum height is found as 1.34 nm, which confirm the graphene structure of RhB-GQDs is few layers configuration (1–2 layers).

The elemental compositions and function groups of RhB-GQDs were further characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) ( Fig. 2) and FT-IR ( Fig. 3). The XPS survey spectrum of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs is illustrated in Fig. 2(a), including 3 peaks at 285 eV, 530 eV, 399 eV, which corresponding to C1s, O1s and N1s, respectively. This suggests that the RhB-GQDs are mainly composed of carbon, oxygen and nitrogen. As shown in Fig. 2(b), the deconvolution of high-resolution scans of C 1s reveals the presence of three different peaks, corresponding to three different chemical states, C˭C (284.4 eV), C-N/C-O (285.8 eV) and C˭O (287.2 eV), respectively [29]. The peak near 284.4 eV indicates the presence of graphitic C in the lattice of RhB-GQDs [30]. Meanwhile, the peak at 285.8 eV can be identified as the sp
3 bonded carbon with oxygen (C-O) and nitrogen (C-N) [31]. It is worth noting that the peak near 278.2 eV corresponds to the double bond between carbon and oxygen (C˭O) [32]. The introduction of rhodanmine B can enhance the content of N doping in graphite lattice. According to the total XPS survey spectrum, the element analysis result shows that the N content is about 16.06%. The high N content of RhB-GQDs is partly form the contribution of N doping, and the another part is the contribution of rhodamine B. As shown in Fig. 2(c), there are three peaks can be observed at 399.2 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.1 eV in the high-resolution scan of N1s, which can be assigned to the pyrrolic N (C-N-C), N-H groups and –N-C˭O bond [25], respectively. It indicates that amide linkage between rhodamine B and graphene quantum dots. The high-resolution O 1s spectra of the RhB-GQDs shows two peaks at 530.5 eV and 532.5 eV, which corresponding to C˭O and C-OH on the surface. This indicates that the presence of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface and edge of RhB-GQDs [32].

FT-IR was applied to characterize the functional groups in the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs. As shown in Fig. 3, the peaks at 1554 cm−1 and 1655 cm−1 is corresponding to the C˭C and C˭O stretching [30,32], respectively. A broad absorption peak at 3417 cm‒1 is identified as the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H. The existence of hydrophilic hydroxyl, carboxylic and amino groups on the surface of RhB-GQDs are favored to give it good hydrophilic properties and stability in water. The N doping on RhB-GQDs is ascertained as the stretching vibration of C˭N at 1474 cm−1. The as-synthesized RhB-GQDs are formed by carbonization of critic acid molecules. According to above analysis, abundant carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of RhB-GQDs, which are related to the selectivity and sensitivity of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs to mercury ions.

The optical properties of RhB-GQDs were investigated by UV–vis and fluorescence spectroscopy as shown in Fig. 4, respectively. It is obvious that the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs exhibit two characteristic absorption peaks at 235 nm and 350 nm, respectively. The strong absorption peak at 350 nm that is due to the n–π* transition of C˭O and C˭N usually observed in doped system [33]. The absorption band at 235 nm is contributed from π–π* transition [34,35]. Furthermore, the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs exhibit blue fluorescence under UV lamp irradiation (Fig. 4(a), black line). The fluorescence intensity of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs as a function of excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 4(b). When the wavelength is increased from 250 nm to 400 nm, only small change occurs in the emission peak position. The quantum yield of RhB-GQDs is calculated to be 41.6% using the quinine sulfate (54.6%, 0.1 M H2SO4) as reference.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Influence of pH towards the fluorescence behavior of RhB-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The pH effect on the fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs in the presence and absence of 1 μM Hg2+ was investigated. A wide pH range from 2 to 10 was carried out ( Fig. 5). The dilute NaOH and H3PO4 solution (0.1 M) were utilized to adjust the pH. As the pH values reach to 2.0, the fluorescence intensity is weak. After that the fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs is increased gradually with the increase of pH values from 2 to 6. When the pH value is higher than 6, the fluorescence intensity remains nearly. This phenomenon can be ascribed to the protonation and deprotonation of amino and carboxylic functional groups on the surface of RhB-GQDs at acidic or alkaline conditions [36]. Under acidic condition, the carboxylic and amino groups are bonded with proton (H+), which changes the electron distribution on the surface of RhB-GQDs, leading to the aggregation of RhB-GQDs [37]. Thus, the fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs is weak under acidic condition. In alkaline conditions, the carboxylic groups in the surface of RhB-GQDs are gradually deprotonated. The deprotonation process of carboxylic groups can form negative charge protective shells on the surface of RhB-GQDs, which make the fluorescence intensity remains nearly [38]. Before the addition of Hg2+ ions, the effect of pH change can be described as changes of electron distribution on the surface. These changes are related to the deprotonation and protonation processes of functional groups on the RhB-GQDs surface.

As illustrated in Fig. 5, after the addition of Hg2+ the fluorescence quenching of RhB-GQDs increases with the pH value change from 2 to 7, but decreases after the pH value exceeding 7. The difference of fluorescence quenching under acidic and alkaline conditions can be illustrated by the protonation and deprotonation of amino and carboxyl groups on the surface of RhB-GQDs. In that case, only few amount of Hg2+ can contact with RhB-GQDs, reducing the fluorescence quenching. Considering the pH of real sample water is close to 7 and the best quenching efficiency, the following fluorescent study was recorded at pH 7.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+ using RhB-GQDs as fluorescence probe
ce:section.ce:para: After the characterization of RhB-GQDs, the fluorescent intensity of RhB-GQDs was recorded to investigate the sensitivity of RhB-GQDs toward Hg2+. The sensitivity and linear response range of RhB-GQDs-based system were assessed by measuring the fluorescence intensity quenching of the RhB-GQDs upon addition of Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 6(a), the fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs is negatively correlated with the concentration of Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity is effectively quenched upon addition of Hg2+ confirming the validity of the fluorescent detection of Hg2+.

Fig. 6(b) shows the concentration of Hg2+ as a function of fluorescence quenching ratio (F/F0, F and F0 refer to the fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs at 437 nm in the presence and absence of Hg2+, respectively). Fig. 6(c) and (d) show the linear correlation on the concentrations of Hg2+ in the range of 50–1000 nM and 1–10 nM, respectively. A good linearity can be obtained in the Hg2+ concentrations of 0–10 nM with an excellent correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.997) and liner regression equation I/I0 = 0.9857–0.00322 [Hg2+]. Consequently, the detection limit (LOD) for Hg2+ is calculated to be 0.16 nM with a signal-to-noise ratio of 3, which is lower than some other methods for detection of Hg2+ [39,40]. In addition, another detection limit of 40.87 nM can be estimated upon a higher Hg2+ concentration range of 50–1000 nM (Fig. 6(c)). Chinese National Standers rule clearly stipulate that the maximum safe concentration of Hg2+ in drinking water shall not exceed 0.001 mg L−1 (4.98 nM), and the total mercury content in industrial effluents shall be not greater than 0.05 mg L−1 (249 nM) [41]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency also designates the Maximum Contaminant Levels (MCLs) for drinking water. The threshold limit on the amount of mercury is 0.002 mg L−1 (9.96 nM) [42]. Accordingly, the linear dynamic response range of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs in this paper can meet the requirements of mercury detection in both drinking water and industrial effluents, and has potential application in real sample detection. Table 1 lists the analytical performance of Hg2+ detection system by various fluorescence probes. It can be seen from Table 1 that the detection limit obtained with the present method is competitive as the reported data listed in the table.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanisms of the Hg2+ sensing properties of RhB-GQDs
ce:section.ce:para: Dynamic and static quenching are two commonly quenching process for graphene quantum dots as fluorescent sensor [43]. In a dynamic quenching process, the fluorescence quenching is caused by the non-radiative electron transfer between quencher and the excited state of fluorescence substance. And, the reaction between quencher and fluorescence materials form non-fluorescence complex leads to static quenching. According to the previous reports, the lifetime of semiconductor colloids decreases can be attributed to the electron-transfer, and the quenching can be predicted to be dynamic quenching [43,44]. Fig. 7(a) depicts the fluorescence lifetime of RhB-GQDs in the presence and absence of Hg2+ ions. The fluorescence lifetime of RhB-GQDs decrease from 15.58 to 13.30 ns after the addition of 10 μM Hg2+, indicating the electron transfer between RhB-GQDs and Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 7(b), there are no obvious new absorption peak generate after the addition of Hg2+ ions. The absorption threshold and peak position do not change after the addition of 1 μM and 3 μM Hg2+ solution, which suggest that Hg2+ ions do not react with RhB-GQDs to form stable metal complexes. Therefore, non-fluorescence electron transfers from the excited states of RhB-GQDs to Hg2+ is the main reason for fluorescence quenching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective fluorescence response of RhB-GQDs for Hg2+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: In order to assess the selectivity of RhB-GQDs toward different metal ions, 50 µM possible interfering substance including Ag+, K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Co2+, Cd2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, Ni2+, and Pb2+, were added into the RhB-GQDs solution to record the change in fluorescence intensity. Fig. 8 shows that these 15 different cations have no obvious quenching effect on RhB-GQDs. In addition, the concentrations of these cations in 50 µM are far exceeded the safety requirement. These results clearly demonstrate excellent selectivity of RhB-GQDs for Hg2+ detection in a wide range of cations.

The high selectivity of RhB-GQDs towards Hg2+ may be attributed to the synergistic effect of N and O functional groups on RhB-GQDs surface. Some reports have suggested that the sensitive recognition mechanism results from the special coordination interaction between cations and functional groups (amino, hydroxyl and carboxyl groups) on the surface of the GQDs [45–47]. The fluorescence quenching of as-synthesized RhB-GQDs requires sufficient contact between the quencher and the fluorescent substance. As shown in Fig. 9, rhodamine B attached onto the surface of RhB-GQDs form a protective shell. Heavy metal cations need to pass through rhodamine B protective shell before the electron transfer occurs. K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Na+ are the common ions in drinking water and rivers. They have weak affinity with amino and carboxyl group at neutral condition. As a result, it is difficult for K+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, and Na+ ions to pass through the rhodamine B protective shell and quench the fluorescence of RhB-GQDs. Among interfering metallic cations, Ag+, Co2+, Cr3+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, and Ni2+ can coordinate with hydroxyl and amino groups to form complex in the neutral reaction medium [48]. Rhodamine B shell can prevent aforementioned cation from contacting with RhB-GQD. In addition, nitrogen atoms in graphene sheets of RhB-GQDs can increase the electron density of carboxyl groups, and reduce the polarity of the O˭H bond in the carboxyl groups. Polarity decreasing process of O˭H bond reduce the ability to form covalent bonds between aforementioned metallic ions and the carboxyl on the surface of RhB-GQDs [48]. Therefore, only Hg2+ ions have good affinity with carboxylic groups of rhodamine B and RhB-GQDs, resulting effective contact. The contact between quantum dots and mercury ions is conducive to the non-radiative electrons and energy transfer, leading to dynamic quenching of fluorescence.

The applicability of the as-synthesized RhB-GQDs was evaluated by recording the fluorescence intensity change after the addition of Hg2+ ions in drinking water. Fig. 10 shows the change in fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs in drinking water after the addition of 50 nM, 100 nM, and 300 nM Hg2+. The fluorescence intensity of RhB-GQDs was quenched by Hg2+ ions. As shown in Table 2, the recovery of Hg2+ in drinking water samples was calculated. The recoveries of 114 ± 4%, 109 ± 2.1% and 107 ± 6.7% can be observed in low concentration of 50–300 nM Hg2+ in drinking water. The results indicate that RhB-GQDs can be served as a fluorescent sensor for the Hg2+ detection in drinking water.

In summary, water soluble RhB-GQDs with few layers configuration were synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal method, and applied to sensitive and selective Hg2+ detection. Abundant carboxyl and amino groups on the surface of RhB-GQDs lead to good affinity toward Hg2+, contribute to the non-fluorescence electron transfer between RhB-GQDs and Hg2+. In addition, two stage linear ranges have potential application on mercury detection in drinking water and industrial effluents. A linear range of 1–10 nM with LOD of 0.16 nM is obtained for nanomolar level Hg2+ detection. As a result of N atoms in graphene quantum dots lattice, reduce the ability of interfering metal cations to form covalent bond with carboxyl. The real sample analysis obtained in this study indicate that the RhB-GQDs-based sensor can be used as a promising candidate for a low cost, simple, rapid and sensitive Hg2+ detection.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Department of Science and Technology of Yunnan Province via the Key Project for the Science and Technology (Grant No. 2017FA025), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 61761047 and 41876055) and Program for Innovative Research Team (in Science and Technology) in University of Yunnan Province.
